item.title: Iceland Ousts Switzerland as World’s Priciest Nation, Union Says
item.description: Iceland is again the world’s most expensive country, surpassing Switzerland for the first time in years, calculations by a local trade union showed.

item.title: China’s Crop Belt Faces Flood Risk as Heavy Rains Arrive Early
item.description: Heavy rains are arriving earlier and lasting longer across several parts of China this year, raising risks for agriculture and disaster management.

item.title: Africa Startups Turn Inward as US AI Boom Drains Venture Capital
item.description: African startups are rewriting their funding playbook as the global artificial intelligence boom pulls venture capital toward the US, forcing emerging markets to compete for cash.

item.title: A 1,700% Jump Turns Retail Trader Favorite Sivers Into Short Bet
item.description: A small Swedish semiconductor firm that’s soared about 1,700% this year has become one of the country’s most-shorted stocks.

item.title: Carson Block Is Rethinking Indian Fund Plan With Focus on AI
item.description: Carson Block said artificial intelligence is a key reason Muddy Waters Capital LLC is rethinking plans for a long-short fund in India, saying his firm is “going back to the lab” on the idea.

item.title: Record Spend on Data Centers Drives Australia Investment Growth
item.description: Record investment in data centers in Australia is providing much-needed support for the economy that’s feeling the strain from the global energy crunch and higher interest rates.

item.title: Goldman Desk Sees Korea Leveraged ETFs as Volatility Accelerator
item.description: South Korea’s leveraged exchange-traded funds tied to chipmakers may deepen concentration in the equity market and amplify volatility, according to Goldman Sachs Group Inc.’s sales desk.

item.title: Buyers of US Cooking Gas Cancel Cargoes as Freight Costs Soar
item.description: Some buyers of US liquefied petroleum gas canceled shipments that would typically be bound for Asia following a surge in freight rates sparked by the conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Why the Market Isn’t Working as Trump's Guardrail
item.description: Lack of volatility is taking off pressure to reach a Strait of Hormuz deal.

item.title: South Africa Poised for First Rate Hike Since 2023 on Iran War
item.description: South Africa’s central bank is set to raise borrowing costs for the first time in three years as policymakers seek to combat inflationary pressures stemming from the Iran war, including sharply higher oil prices.

item.title: The World Cup Is a Petri Dish
item.description: Millions of international visitors are coming to North America for the competition, and the odds are that quite a few will be unwell.

item.title: The Top 25 African Startups to Watch in 2026
item.description: These companies are building solutions in environments where infrastructure or systems have failed to deliver.

item.title: Oil Investments to Drop for Third Year on War Shock, IEA Says
item.description: Global investments in oil projects are due to fall for the third year in a row, as the supply shock from the Middle East conflict shifts priorities to new trade routes and other energy sources.

item.title: Treasuries Resume Decline as US Strikes on Iran Drive Oil Higher
item.description: Benchmark Treasuries retreated for the first session in six as renewed clashes in the Persian Gulf spurred gains in oil prices and fueled concern over faster inflation.

item.title: Industrial Metals Decline as Gulf Strikes Dampen Deal Optimism
item.description: Copper extended losses alongside other industrial metals after the US carried out fresh strikes in the Persian Gulf and a deal to end the war with Iran remained elusive.

item.title: Oasis Says Japan Proposal on Shareholders Hurts Retail Investors
item.description: Japan’s plan to tighten the threshold for shareholder proposals would hurt retail investors while having little impact on institutional activist investors, said Seth Fischer, the founder of Oasis Management Co.

item.title: Japan Plans ‘Bridging Bonds’ to Fund Investment Initiatives
item.description: Japan plans to use so-called bridging bonds to help finance Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s investment plans, the government’s top spokesperson said, a development that comes as investors are already concerned about more debt issuance.

item.title: Chinese Coal Disaster to Ripple Through Steel, Power, Chemicals
item.description: The Shanxi coal disaster is likely to hit Chinese output in the short term, raising costs for steelmakers, power plants and chemicals manufacturers.

item.title: New Zealand Lowers Issuance Target, Easing Worries on Debt Binge
item.description: New Zealand unexpectedly lowered its borrowing plans, offering some relief to investors increasingly concerned about rising government debt globally.

item.title: New Zealand 2026 Budget: Who Are the Winners and Losers
item.description: It’s an election year in New Zealand, but the nation’s annual budget contained few of the usual sweeteners designed to win over undecided voters.

item.title: Palm Oil Titan Wilmar Dives Most in Six Years on Indonesia Probe
item.description: Wilmar International Ltd. fell the most in almost six years after the Indonesian government named the palm oil titan as one of the companies being probed for suspected export abuses.

item.title: New Zealand Government Sees Earlier Return to Budget Surplus
item.description: The New Zealand government will head into this year’s election facing a deeper budget deficit and weaker growth, but with the promise of an earlier return to surplus predicated on an economic recovery starting in 2027.

item.title: Korea’s Hanwha Offers Rockets in Bid for Canada Submarine Deal
item.description: As Canada prepares to award a rich contract to build as many as 12 submarines, Hanwha Corp. of South Korea is sweetening its bid by offering to help in another realm: space.

item.title: Nomura Analyst Says Near-Term BOJ Hike Still Up in Air on Iran
item.description: Market indicators suggest that traders are nearly convinced that the Bank of Japan will raise interest rates in June or July, but a Nomura group analyst isn’t so sure.

item.title: China Markets $885 Million Green Sovereign Bond in Hong Kong
item.description: China is selling as much as 6 billion yuan ($885 million) of green sovereign bonds in Hong Kong on Thursday, returning to the international ESG debt market a year after its debut in London.

item.title: China Chip Giant CXMT’s IPO Revives Memories of Past Market Tops
item.description: The buzz surrounding ChangXin Memory Technologies Ltd.’s anticipated IPO is also triggering anxiety in parts of China’s market, where investors recall earlier mega-deals that absorbed vast amounts of liquidity and marked turning points for equities.

item.title: China’s Declining Appetite for Oil Laid Bare by Iran War
item.description: Chinese imports of crude oil are set to drop to levels not seen since the pandemic, as the war in Iran reveals the extent to which demand has disappeared and may not be coming back.

item.title: BOK Holds Rates Steady Amid Uncertain Outlook for Policy
item.description: South Korea’s central bank kept its benchmark interest rate unchanged Thursday, maintaining a cautious stance as policymakers weighed rising inflation pressure from higher oil prices against uncertainty surrounding the broader economic outlook.

item.title: AI Boom Fuels Record $14.5 Billion in Taiwan Tech Firm Borrowing
item.description: Taiwanese tech firms have completed a record $14.5 billion of debt deals so far this year, as they race to secure financing to meet soaring demand for artificial intelligence capacity.

item.title: Japan Intervention Data Eyed as Yen Hovers Near 160 Per Dollar
item.description: Currency traders are counting down to Friday for official data from Japan’s finance ministry that will show how aggressively authorities intervened to support the yen over the past month.
